questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of integer solutions to $||x|-2020| < 5$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & ||x|-2020| < 5 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-5 < |x|-2020 < 5 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2015 < |x| < 2025 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-2025 < x < -2015 \text { OR } 2015 < x < 2025 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=-2024,-2023, \ldots . .,-2016 ; \\ & \text { OR } x=2016,2017, \ldots ., 2024 \\ & \text { Number of integer solutions }=18\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the largest 2-digit number $N$ which is divisible by 4 , such that all integral powers of $N$ end with $N$.
questions.solution:
If given condition is satisfied for power 2 (i.e. for square), then it will hold for all (+ve) integral powers.
Let the number $N$ be " $a b$ ".
Units place remains same on squaring $b=0$ or 6
If $b=0$, then on squaring even ten's place will become 0 . So $b=0$ is rejected.
$\Rightarrow b=6$
$N$ is divisible by 4 .
Possibilities of $N$ are $16,36,56,76,96$.
Among these only 76 satisfies the required condition
$\Rightarrow N=76$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: The prime numbers $a, b$ and $c$ are such that $a+b^2 =4 c^2$. Determine the sum of all possible values of $a+b+c$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \because 4 c^2-b^2=a \\
& \Rightarrow(2 c-b)(2 c+b)=a \\
& \Rightarrow 2 c-b=1 \text { and } 2 c+b=\mathrm{a} \\
& \Rightarrow 4 c=a+1 \text { and } 2 b=a-1
\end{aligned}$
If $c \geq 5$ than $c$ must be of the type $6 \lambda \pm 1$ for $\lambda \in N$ Let $c=6 \lambda \pm 1$, then $a=24 \lambda+3$ or $24 \lambda-5 \because 24 \lambda+3$ is not a prime, so $c=6 \lambda-1$ and $a=24 \lambda-5$ then $b=\frac{24 \lambda-6}{2}=12 \lambda-3$, which is not a prime.

So $c$ cannot be greater than 5 . If $c=3$ then $a=7$ and $b=5$ and If $c=2$ then $a=7$ and $b=3$ Sum of possible values of $a+b+c=31$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: If $x$ and $y$ are positive integers such that $(x-4) (x-10)=2^y$, find the maximum possible value of $x+y$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Let } y=a+b \\
& (x-4)(x-10)=2^a \cdot 2^b \\
& \therefore \quad x=2^a+4 \text { or } 2^b+10 \text { (vice-versa). } \\
& \therefore \quad x=2^a+4=2^b+10 .
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2^a-2^b=6 . \\ & \Rightarrow a=3 \text { and } b=1 . \quad \therefore \quad y=4 \text { and } x=12 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=16 .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A five digit number $n=\overline{a b c d e}$ is such that when divided respectively by $2,3,4,5,6$ the remainders are $a, b, c, d, e$. What is the remainder when $n$ is divided by 100 ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \because a \in\{0,1\}, b \in\{0,1,2\}, c \in\{0,1,2,3\}, d \in\{0, \\ & 1,2,3,4\}, \quad e \in\{0,1,2,3,4,5\} \end{aligned}$ $\because n$ is a five digit number, so $a=1$ and $n$ must be odd. When $n$ is divided by 5 the remainder is $e$ or $e-5$. So either $d=e$ or $(d, e)=(0,5)$ (i) When $d=e$ then $(d, e)=(1,1)$ or $(3,3)$
de c (divisible by 4) b (divisible by 3)
11 3 0,1,2
33 1 Not possible
So $n=10311$ or 11311 or 12311 Out of which $n=11311$ is correct when we check the condition of divided by 6 . (ii) When $(d, e)=(0,5)$ then $c=1, b$ is not possible. So $n=11311$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: For a natural number $n$, let $n^{\prime}$ denote the number obtained by deleting zero digits, if any. (For example, if $n=260, n^{\prime}=26$; if $n=2020$, $n^{\prime}=22$.) Find the number of 3 -digit numbers n for which $n^{\prime}$ is a divisor of $n$, different from $n$.
questions.solution:
$\because n^{\prime} \neq n$ so atleast one digit of a must be zero
Let $n=\overline{a b c}$
Case I. If only $c=0 \Rightarrow \overline{a b} \mid \overline{a b c}$
$\Rightarrow \overline{a b} \mid 10 \overline{a b}$ (always true)
Number of numbers $n=9 \times 9=81$

Case II. If only $b=0 \Rightarrow \overline{a c} \mid \overline{a b c}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 10 a+c \mid 100 a+c \\
& \Rightarrow 10 a+c \mid 10 a-8 c
\end{aligned}$

So either $10 a=8 c$ or $8 c-10 a=\lambda(10 a+c)$ for some natural number $\lambda$.

When $10 a=8 c \Rightarrow 5 a=4 c \Rightarrow(a, c)=(4,5)$
When $c(8-\lambda)=10 a(\lambda+1) \Rightarrow c=a\left[\frac{90}{8-\lambda}-10\right]$
for $\lambda=1, c=a\left(\frac{20}{7}\right)$ (Not possible)
for $\lambda=2, c=5 a \Rightarrow(a, c)=(1,5)$
for $\lambda=3, c=8 a \Rightarrow(a, c)=(1,8)$
for $\lambda=4, c=a\left(\frac{25}{2}\right)$ (Not possible)
and no further value of $\lambda$ is needed to be checked
Number of numbers $n=3$, which are 405,105 and 108.

Case III. If both $b=c=0$, then all values of $n$ are acceptable.

Number of numbers $n=9$
So total number of possible values of $n=93$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: What is the least positive integer by which $2^5 \cdot 3^6 \cdot 4^3 \cdot 5^3 \cdot 6^7$ should be multiplied so that, the product is a perfect square?
questions.solution:
Given number $N=2^5 \cdot 3^6 \cdot 4^3 \cdot 5^3 \cdot 6^7$
$\begin{aligned}
& N=2^{5+6+7} \cdot 3^{6+7} \cdot 5^3 \\
& N=2^{18} \cdot 3^{13} \cdot 5^3
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Required number }=3^1 \cdot 5^1=15$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the sum of all positive integers $n$ for which $\left|2^n+5^n-65\right|$ is a perfect square.
questions.solution:
$\left|2^n+5^n-65\right|$ has unit digit same as $2^n$
i.e., $2,4,8,6, \ldots \ldots$ for $n=1,2,3,4, \ldots$
Also for perfect square it shall be 4 or 6
Hence $n$ can be even only
for $n=2 \quad\left|2^2+5^2-65\right|=36$
$n=4 \quad\left|2^4+5^4-65\right|=576$
Also $\left(K^2+1\right)^2-K^2=2 K+1$
or $K^2+(2 K+1)=(K+1)^2$
Let $\left|\frac{5^n}{K^2}+\frac{2^n-65}{2 K+1}\right|$ to be $(K+1)^2$
i.e., for $n=6$
$K=\left(5^3\right) \Rightarrow 2 K+1=251$
but $2^6-65=-1$
for $n=8$
$K=5^4 \Rightarrow 2 K+1=626$
but $2^8-65 \Rightarrow 191$
for $n=10$
$K=5^5 \Rightarrow 2 K+1=3126$
but $2^{10}-65 \Rightarrow 959$
The difference increases as $2\left(5^x\right)+1$ rises faster than $2^x-65$

Hence only $n=2$ and 4 are possible

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A total fixed amount of $N$ thousand rupees is given to three persons $A, B, C$, every year, each being given an amount proportional to her age. In the first year, A got half the total amount. When the sixth payment was made, $A$ got six-seventh of the amount that she had in the first year; $B$ got Rs. 1000 less than that she had in the first year; and $C$ got twice of that she had in the first year. Find $N$.
questions.solution:
Let total amount given each year be $N_1$, and the respective ages of persons $A, B$ and $C$ are $a, b$ and $c$ years respectively.
$\therefore$ The ratio of initial distribution $=\mathrm{a}: \mathrm{b}: \mathrm{c}$.
If $k$ is the ratio coefficient then
$a k+b k+c k=N_1 \Rightarrow k=\frac{N_1}{a+b+c}$
A.T.Q: $\frac{a N_1}{a+b+c}=\frac{N_1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{a}{a+b+c}=\frac{1}{2}$...(i)
and $\frac{b+c}{a+b+c}=\frac{1}{2}$...(ii)

When $6^{\text {th }}$ payment was made then ratio of distribution is $a+5: b+5: c+5$.
$\therefore(a+5) k_1+(b+5) k_1+(c+5) k_1=N$
$\therefore k_1=\frac{N_1}{a+b+c+15}$

Again A.T.Q: $\frac{N_1(a+5)}{a+b+c+15}=\frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{2}$...(iii)
$\frac{N_1(b+5)}{a+b+c+15}=\frac{b N_1}{a+b+c}-1000$...(iv)
$\text { and } \frac{N_1(c+5)}{a+b+c+15}=\frac{2 c N_1}{a+b+c}$...(v)

From equation (i) and (iii)
$\frac{a+5}{2 a+15}=\frac{3}{7} \Rightarrow a=10 .$

From (ii) we get $b+c=10$
$\therefore a+b+c=20$

From equation (v) we get : $c=2$ then $b=8$.
and from equation (iv), we get
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{13 N_1}{35}=\frac{8 N_1}{20}-1000 \Rightarrow N_1=35000 \\ & \therefore N=35 \text { thousand }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: For any real number t , let $[\mathrm{t}]$ denote the largest integer $\leq \mathrm{t}$. Suppose that N is the greatest integer such that $[\sqrt{[\sqrt{[\sqrt{\mathrm{N}}]}]}=4$. Find the sum of digit of N .
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & {[\sqrt{[\sqrt{N}]}]=4} \\ & 4 \leq \sqrt{[\sqrt{\sqrt{N}}]} < 5 \\ & 16 \leq[\sqrt{[\sqrt{N}]}] < 25 \\ & 16 \leq \sqrt{[\sqrt{N}]} < 25 \\ & 256 \leq[\sqrt{N}] < 625 \\ & 256 \leq \sqrt{N} < 625 \\ & (256)^2 \leq N < (625)^2 \\ & N_{\text {max. }}=390624 \\ & \text { sum of digit }=24 \text { Ans. }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of ordered pairs $(a, b)$ such that $a, b \in\{10,11, \ldots, 29,30\}$ and $\operatorname{GCD}(a, b)+\operatorname{LCM}(a, b)=a+b$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& g+l=a+b \\
& g+\frac{a b}{g}=a+b \\
& g^2+(a+b) g+a b=0 \\
& (g-a)(g-b)=0 \\
& g=a, b
\end{aligned}$

For $a=b$, there will be 21 cases.
If $a=10, b$ may be 20 or 30 as well and vice-versa.
$\left.\begin{array}{ll}a=11, & b=22 \\ a=12, & b=24 \\ a=13, & b=26 \\ a=14, & b=28 \\ a=15, & b=30\end{array}\right] \times 2=10$ ways

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
For a positive integer $n$, let $\langle n\rangle$ denote the perfect square integer closest to $n$. For example, $\langle 74\rangle=81$, $\langle 18\rangle=16$. If $N$ is the smallest positive integer such that
$\langle 91\rangle \cdot\langle 120\rangle \cdot\langle 143\rangle \cdot\langle 180\rangle \cdot\langle N\rangle=91.120 .143 .180 \cdot N$
find the sum of the squares of the digits of $N$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \because \quad 10^2 \cdot 11^2 \cdot 12^2 \cdot 13^2 \cdot\langle N\rangle=91 \cdot 120 \cdot 143 \cdot 180 \cdot N \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 22\langle N\rangle=21 N
\end{aligned}$
$\because\langle N\rangle$ is a perfect square. So, $\langle N\rangle$ must have a factor $21^2$.

For least positive value of $N$, let $\langle N\rangle=21^2$
So range of $N$ will be, $21^2-20 \leq N \leq 21^2+21$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad 22.21^2=21 . N \\
& \Rightarrow \quad N=21.22=462
\end{aligned}$

Sum of squares of digits $=4^2+6^2+2^2=56$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of pairs $(a, b)$ of natural numbers such that $b$ is a 3 -digit number, $a+1$ divides $b-1$ and $b$ divides $a^2+a+2$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 100 \leq b \leq 999 \\
& \quad(b-1)=\lambda(a+1)....(i)
\end{aligned}$
and $a^2+a+2=\mu b$...(ii)
$\therefore a^2+a+2=b, 2 b, 3 b,$
$\_\_\_\_$
i.e., $a \geq 14$ hence
for $a=14 \quad$ gives $a^2+a+2=212$
i.e., $b=106,212$
but $(a, b)=(14,106)$ satisfy (i)
Similarly $(16,137),(18,171)$ $\_\_\_\_$ $(44,991)$ as $b \leq 999$.

Hence, 16 orderd pairs.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Positive integers $a, b, c$ satisfy $\frac{a b}{a-b}=c$. What is the largest possible value of $a+b+c$ not exceeding 99?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \because \frac{1}{b}=\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{c} \\
& \Rightarrow b < \min (a, c)
\end{aligned}$

Case I: When $a=c$, then $b=\frac{a}{2}$
$\Rightarrow b \in$ even
then, $a+b+c=5 b$
$\Rightarrow(a+b+c)_{\max }=95$
Case II: When $a \neq c$
Let $a=\lambda a_1$ and $c=\lambda c_1$ where $\operatorname{gcd}\left(a_1, c_1\right)=1$
So, $\frac{1}{b}=\frac{a_1+c_1}{\lambda a_1 c_1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad b=\left(\frac{\lambda}{a_1+c_1}\right) a_1 \cdot c_1$

So, we must select $\lambda$ such that $\left(a_1+c_1\right) \mid \lambda$
If we check for maximum value of $(a+b+c)=99$ then $\left(a_1+c_1\right)$ must be a divisor of 99 .

It is possible when $\left(a_1, c_1\right)=(1,2)$, then $\lambda$ is divisible by 3 . (say $\lambda=3 \mu$ )
$\begin{aligned}
& a=3 \mu, c=6 \mu \text { and } b=2 \mu \\
& \Rightarrow a+b+c=11 \mu
\end{aligned}$

If we take $\mu=9,(a+b+c)_{\max }=99$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
Let $m, n$ be natural numbers such that
$m+3 n-5=5 \mathrm{LCM}(m, n)-11 \mathrm{GCD}(m, n)$

Find the maximum possible value of $m+n$.
questions.solution:
Let G. C. D. of $(m, n)=d$
Then for some positive coprime integers $x$ and $y$
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& m=d x \text { and } n=d y \\
\because & m+3 n-5=2 \text { LCM }(m, n)-11 \text { GCD }(m, n) \\
\therefore & d x+3 d y-5=2 d x y-11 d \\
\text { or, } & d(x+3 y-2 x y+11)=5
\end{array}$

Now, to maximize the sum $m+n, d$ must be 5
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad x+3 y-2 x y+11=1 \\
& \text { or } \quad x+3 y-2 x y+10=0 \\
& \text { or } \quad(2 x-3)(2 y-1)=23
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Case } I: 2 x-3=1 \text { and } 2 y-1=23 \\
& \therefore \quad x=2 \text { and } y=12
\end{aligned}$

This is not possible as $x, y$ are coprime
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Case II : } 2 x-3=23 \text { and } 2 y-1=1 \\
& \therefore \quad x=13 \text { and } y=1 \\
& \therefore \quad m+n=(13+1) \times 5=70
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $X$ be the set of all even positive integers $n$ such that the measure of the angle of some regular polygon is $n$ degrees. Find the number of elements in $X$.
questions.solution:
Let number of sides of polygon be $P$.
$\therefore  \frac{(P-2) \cdot 180^{\circ}}{P}=n^{\circ}$
then $P(180-n)=360$
$\therefore  P=\frac{360}{180-n}$
But $n=2 k$ then $P=\frac{180}{90-k}$
Hence possible values of $(90-k)$ are $1,2,3,4,5,6,9,10,12,15,18,20,30,36,45$ and 60
$\therefore 16$ such polygons are possible.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $x, y$ be positive integers such that $x^4=(x-1)\left(y^3-23\right)-1$. Find the maximum possible value of $x+y$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \sqrt{4 n+r}=k \in I \\
& \Rightarrow  4 n+r=k^2 \\
& \frac{\left(x^4+1\right)}{(x-1)}=y^3-23, x, y \in I
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Since, } x \neq 1 \Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned}
& x \geq 2 \\
& y^3-23 \in I
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow  \frac{x^4+1}{x-1} \in I$
Let, $x-1=p$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow  \frac{(1+p)^4+1}{p} \\
& \Rightarrow  \frac{\left(a_4 p^4+a_3 p^3+\ldots . . a_1 p+2\right)}{p}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \frac{2}{p} \Rightarrow p \text { divides } 2 \\
\Rightarrow & p=\{-2,-1,1,2\} \\
\Rightarrow & x \in\{-1,0,2,3\}
\end{array}$
but $x \geq 2 \Rightarrow x=2$ or 3
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { If } x=2 \quad 2^4=1 \times\left(y^3-23\right)-1 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(17+23)=y^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad y \notin I
\end{aligned}$
If $x=3 \quad\left(3^4+1\right)=2\left(y^3-23\right)=y=4$
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y=7$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
The positive real numbers $a, b, c$ satisfy:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{a}{2 b+1}+\frac{2 b}{3 c+1}+\frac{3 c}{a+1}=1 \\
& \frac{1}{a+1}+\frac{1}{2 b+1}+\frac{1}{3 c+1}=2
\end{aligned}$
What is the value of $\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c} ?$
questions.solution:
$\frac{3 c}{a+1}+\frac{a}{2 b+1}+\frac{2 b}{3 c+1}=1$......(i)
$\frac{1}{a+1}+\frac{1}{2 b+1}+\frac{1}{3 c+1}=2$......(ii)
Add (i) and (ii)
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 c+1}{a+1}+\frac{a+1}{2 b+1}+\frac{2 b+1}{3 c+1}=3$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \text { A.M. } \geq \text { G.M. } \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{\frac{3 c+1}{a+1}+\frac{a+1}{2 b+1}+\frac{2 b+1}{3 c+1}}{3}\right) \geq(1)^{\frac{1}{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 c+1}{a+1}=1 \text { and } \frac{2 b+1}{3 c+1}=1 \\ & \quad \frac{a+1}{2 b+1}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(a+1)=(2 b+1)=(3(+1))=k \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{k}+\frac{1}{k}+\frac{1}{k}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{3}{k}=2 \Rightarrow k=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{1}{2}, b=\frac{1}{4}, c=\frac{1}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}=2+4+6=12\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Determine the number of positive integral value of $p$ for which there exists a triangle with side $a, b$ and $c$ which satisfy $a^2+\left(p^2+9\right) b^2+9 c^2-6 a b-6 p b c=0$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& a^2+9 b^2-6 a b+p^2 b^2-6 p b c+9 c^2=0 \\
& (a-3 b)^2+(p b-3 c)^2=0 \\
& a=3 b \quad p b=3 c \\
& \Rightarrow \quad c=\frac{p b}{3}
\end{aligned}$
If $a$ is the largest side
$\begin{aligned}
& b+\frac{p b}{3}>3 b \\
& 3+p>9
\end{aligned}$
$p>6$......(i)
If $c$ is largest side
$3 b+b>\frac{p b}{3}$
$p < 12$......(ii)
so, $p=\{7,8,9,10,11\}$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ Number of positive integral value of $p$ is 5

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of triples of real numbers $(a, b, c)$ such that $a^{20}+b^{20}+c^{20}=a^{24}+b^{24}+c^{24}=1$.
questions.solution:
$a^{20}+b^{20}+c^{20}=a^{24}+b^{24}+c^{24}=1$
If both equation have to satisfy
$\Rightarrow \quad$ Either $a$ or $b$ or $c= \pm 1$
And other have to be 0 .
So, if $a=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad b=c=0$
If $a=-1$
$\Rightarrow \quad b=c=0$
Similarly, for $b$ and $c$ if $b= \pm 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=c=0$
And if $c= \pm 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=b=0$
Total triples $=6$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of elements in the set $\left\{(a, b) \in N: 2 \leq a, b \leq 2023, \log _a(b)+6 \log _b(a)=5\right\}$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \log _a b=t \\ & t+\frac{6}{t}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow  t^2-5 t+6=0 \quad \Rightarrow  t=2 \text { or } 3 \\ & \log _a b=2 \text { or } 3 \Rightarrow  b=a^2 \text { or } a^3 \\ & a^2 \leq 2023 \text { and } a^3 \leq 2023 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a \leq 44.97 \text { and } a \leq 12.64 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a \in\{2, \ldots .44\} \text { and } a \in\{2, \ldots .12\} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(44-2+1)+(12-2+1) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 43+11=54\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive integers such that $\frac{16}{37} < \frac{\alpha}{\beta} < \frac{7}{16}$. Find the smallest possible value of $\beta$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \because \quad \frac{16}{37} < \frac{\alpha}{\beta} < \frac{7}{16} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{37}{16}>\frac{\beta}{\alpha}>\frac{16}{7} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{37}{16} \alpha>\beta>\frac{16}{7} \alpha\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{7} \alpha < \beta-2 \alpha < \frac{5}{16} \alpha, \beta-2 \alpha \in I \text { and lie between }\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{7}, \frac{5}{16} \alpha\right) \equiv\left(\frac{32 \alpha}{112}, \frac{35 \alpha}{112}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(\beta-2 \alpha) \in(0.28 \alpha, 0.31 \alpha) \\ & \text { If } \alpha=10 \Rightarrow(2.8,3.1) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \beta-2 \alpha=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \beta=23\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A contractor has two teams of workers : team A and team B. Team A can complete a job in 12 days and team $B$ can do the same job in 36 days. Team A starts working on the job and team B joins team $A$ after four days. The team $A$ withdraws after two more days. For how many more days should team B work to complete the job ?
questions.solution:
Let total worker in team A be x
Let total worker in team B be y
Given $12 \mathrm{x}=36 \mathrm{y}$
x = 3y

Per worker work done per day be W .
total work $36 \times \mathrm{W} \times \mathrm{y}$
$\begin{aligned}
& 36 W y=6 \times W x+M W y \\
& M=2+\text { Number of more days. } \\
& 36 y=18 y+M y \\
& M=18 y+M y \\
& M=18
\end{aligned}$

Hence 16 more days

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of positive integers $n$ such that $\sqrt{n}+\sqrt{n+1} < 11$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \sqrt{\mathrm{N}}+\sqrt{\mathrm{N}+1} < 11 \\
& \mathrm{~N}=1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8 \ldots \ldots .16 \ldots \ldots 25 \\
& \sqrt{\mathrm{~N}+1}+\sqrt{\mathrm{N}}=11 ...(1)\\
& \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~N}+1}-\sqrt{\mathrm{N}}}=11 \\
& \sqrt{\mathrm{~N}+1}-\sqrt{\mathrm{N}}=\frac{1}{11} ...(2)\\
& 2 \sqrt{\mathrm{~N}+1}=\frac{122}{11} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{~N}+1}=\frac{61}{11} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~N}+1=\frac{3721}{121} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=\frac{3600}{121}=29.75
\end{aligned}$

So 29 values

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A pen costs $₹ 11$ and a notebook costs $₹ 13$. Find the number of ways in which a person can spend exactly ₹ 1000 to buy pens and notebooks.
questions.solution:
Cost of a pen is $₹ 11$
Cost of a notebook is ₹ 13
$\begin{aligned}
& 11 x+13 y=1000 \\
& y=\frac{1000-11 x}{13} \in I \\
& \frac{12-11 x}{13} \in I \Rightarrow \frac{(13-1)-(13 x-2 x)}{13} \in I \\
& \frac{2 x-1}{13} \in I \Rightarrow \frac{12 x-6}{13} \in I \\
& \frac{13 x-(x+6)}{13} \in I \Rightarrow \frac{x+6}{13} \in I \\
& x=13 \lambda-6 \quad(1 \leq x \leq 90) \\
& x \in\{7,20, \ldots \ldots, 85\} \quad\{\lambda=\{1,2, \ldots, 3\}\}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: In a class, the total numbers of boys and girls are in the ratio $4: 3$. On one day it was found that 8 boys and 14 girls were absent from the class and that the number of boys was the square of the number of girls. What is the total number of students in the class?
questions.solution:
Ratio is $4: 3$ therefore
Boys are 4 x
Girls are 3 x
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { given }(4 x-8)=(3 x-14)^2 \\
& 9 x^2+196-84 x=4 x-8 \\
& 9 x^2-88 x+204=0 \\
& x=\frac{88 \pm \sqrt{88^2-4 \times 9 \times 204}}{18} \\
& =\frac{2\left(22 \pm \sqrt{22^2-9 \times 51}\right)}{9} \\
& =\frac{2(22 \pm 5)}{9}=2 \times \frac{27}{9} \text { or } 2 \times \frac{17}{9} \\
& x=6 \text { or } \frac{34}{9}
\end{aligned}$
$7 x=42$ or non integer $\Rightarrow 42$ students

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: How many positive integers less than 1000 have the property that the sum of the digits of each such number is divisible by 7 and the number itself is divisible by 3 ?

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: An integer $n$ is such that $\left[\frac{n}{9}\right]$ is a three digit number with equal digits, and $\left[\frac{n-172}{4}\right]$ is a 4 digit number with the digits $2,0,2,4$ in some order. What is the remainder when $n$ is divided by 100 ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let }\left[\frac{n-172}{4}\right]=k, k \in I \\ & \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{n-172}{4} \in[k, k+1) \\ \quad n-172 \in[4 k, 4 k+4) \\ \quad n \in[4 k+172,4 k+176) \\ \quad \frac{n}{9} \in\left[\frac{4 k+172}{9}, \frac{4 k+176}{9}\right)\end{array} \\ & \text { Now, }\left[\frac{n}{9}\right] \in\{111,222,333, \ldots, 999\} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{4 k+176}{9}>\overline{a a a}>\frac{4 k+172}{9}, \text { for } a \in\{1, \ldots, 9\} \\ & \text { For } a=9 \\ & k \in(2203.75,2204.75) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=2204\end{aligned}$
$\left[\frac{n-172}{4}\right]=2204$......(i)
And $\left[\frac{n}{9}\right]=999$......(ii)
From (i):
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{n-172}{4} \in[2204,2205) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n \in[8988,8992)
\end{aligned}$
From (ii):
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{n}{9} \in[999,1000) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n \in[8991,9000) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n=8991 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 91
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $X$ be the set consisting of twenty positive integers $n, n+2, \ldots, n+38$. The smallest value of $n$ for which any three numbers $a, b, c \in X$, not necessarily distinct, form the sides of an acute-angled triangle is:
questions.solution:
$X=\{n, n+2, \ldots, n+38\}$
$a, b, c \in X$
For any $a, b, c$
(i) Triangle should be formed
(ii) Triangle should be acute
$\rightarrow$ only one angle can be obtuse at max

(i) let $a \leq b \leq c$
$\Rightarrow$ for triangle
$a+b>c$ for all possible combination
$\Rightarrow$ even if $a, b$ are smallest $a=b=n$
$\Rightarrow n+n>n+38$
$\Rightarrow n>38 \Rightarrow$ triangle will form

(ii) now using cosine formula largest side longest angle
$\Rightarrow \cos c=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}>0$ for acute $\Delta$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^2+b^2-c^2>0$ for acute $\Delta \forall a, b, c \in X$
$n^2+(n)^2-(n+38)^2>0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad n^2-76 n-38^2>0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n>91.74 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n=92
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $p, q$ be two-digit numbers neither of which are divisible by 10 . Let $r$ be the four-digit number by putting the digits of $p$ followed by the digits of $q$ (in order). As $p, q$ vary, a computer prints $r$ on the screen if $\operatorname{gcd}(p, q)=1$ and $p+q$ divides $r$. Suppose that the largest number that is printed by the computer is $N$. Determine the number formed by the last two digits of $N$ (in the same order).
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& r=100 r+q \\
& r+q \mid r=100 r+q \\
& \Rightarrow \quad r+q \mid 99 r
\end{aligned}$
But $\operatorname{gcd}(r+q, r)=1$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & r+q \mid 99 \\
\therefore & r+q=33 \text { or } 99
\end{array}$
For $N$ to be maximum $r+q=99$
Where $r=89, q=13$
Answer 13.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: For each positive integer n , consider the highest common factor $\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{n}}$ of the two numbers $\mathrm{n}!+1$ and $(n+1)!$. For $n < 100$, find the largest value of $h_n$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}!+1$ is not divisible by $1,2, \ldots, \mathrm{n}$
$(\mathrm{n}+1)$ ! is divisible by $1,2, \ldots, \mathrm{n}$
so $\mathrm{HCF} \geq \mathrm{n}+1$
also ( $\mathrm{n}+1$ )! is not divisible by $\mathrm{n}+2, \mathrm{n}+3 \ldots$.
so HCF can be $\mathrm{n}+1$ only
Let us start by taking $\mathrm{n}=99$
$\Rightarrow 99!+1$ and 100 !
$\mathrm{HCF}=100$ is not possible as 100 divides 99 !
composite number will not be able to make it
so let us take prime i.e. $\mathrm{n}=97$
now 96 ! +1 and 97 ! are both divisible by 97
so $\mathrm{HCF}=97$
(by Wilson's theorem $(p-1)!+1$ ) is divisible by $p$ )

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the smallest positive integer $n \geq 10$ such that $n+6$ is a prime and $9 n+7$ is a perfect square.
questions.solution:
Since $9 n+7$ is a perfect square
Let us assume that $9 n+7=\mathrm{m}^2$
Also $n+6$ is a prime number $\Rightarrow n+6$ must be odd number $\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}$ also must be an odd number
So Let us assume $\mathrm{n}=2 \mathrm{k}+1$....(ii) where k is an Integer
$\begin{aligned}
& 9(2 k+1)+7=m^2 \\
& \Rightarrow 18 k=m^2-16 \\
& \Rightarrow 18 k=(m+4)(m-4) ...(iii)
\end{aligned}$
Since $18 k$ is even and $m+4$ and $m-4$ both are of some parity $\Rightarrow m$ must also be even. say $m=2 p$, where $p$ is an Integer
Substituting in (iii)
$\begin{aligned}
& 18 k=(2 p+4)(2 p-4)=4(p+2)(p-2) \\
& \Rightarrow 9 k=2(p+2)(p-2)
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}$ must be even, say $\mathrm{k}=2 \ell$ where $\ell$ is an Integer
$\begin{aligned}
& 18 \ell=2(\mathrm{p}+2)(\mathrm{p}-2) \\
& \Rightarrow 9 \ell=(\mathrm{p}+2)(\mathrm{p}-2)
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow p$ must be of the form $9 q+2$ or $9 q-2$, where $q$ is an Integer
First take $p=9 q-2 \Rightarrow m=2(9 q-2)$
Take $\mathrm{q}=1, \mathrm{~m}=2 \times 7=14 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}^2=196$
$9 n+7=196 \Rightarrow 9 n=189 \Rightarrow n=21$
$\Rightarrow n+6=27$ which is not prime
Next take $p=9 q+2 \Rightarrow m=2(9 q+2)$
Take $\mathrm{q}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=2 \times 11=22 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}^2=484$
$9 n+7=484 \Rightarrow 9 n=477 \Rightarrow n=53$
$\Rightarrow n+6=59$ which is a prime
So $\mathrm{n}=53$ is the smallest such number

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $E$ denote the set of all natural numbers $n$ such that $3 < n < 100$ and the set $\{1,2,3, \ldots, n\}$ can be partitioned in to 3 subsets with equal sums. Find the number of elements of $E$.
questions.solution:
$\{1,2 \ldots \ldots \ldots . . n\}$
This set can be partitioned into 3 subsets with equal sums so total sum is divisible by 3 , $\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}$ is divisible by 3 .

So n will be of the form $3 \lambda, 3 \lambda+2$
or for convenience we can take $\mathrm{n}=6 \lambda, 6 \lambda+2,6 \lambda+3,6 \lambda+5$
If $\mathrm{n}=6 \lambda$ then we can group numbers in bundles of 6 .
In each bundle we can select numbers like $1,2,3,4,5,6(16,25,34)$
If $n=6 \lambda+2$ then we can club last bundle of 8 numbers rest can be partitioned and those 8 numbers can be done $1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8(1236,48)$
If $\mathrm{n}=6 \lambda+3$, we can club last nine numbers and rest can be partitioned $1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 (12345,69,78)$
If $n=6 \lambda+5$ we can take last five numbers. Rest can be partitioned $1,2,3,4,5(14,23,5)$
Hence we can select any number of form $6 \lambda(16), 6 \lambda+2(16), 6 \lambda+3(16), 6 \lambda+5(16)$ so total 64 numbers.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $\overline{a b c}$ be a three digit number with nonzero digits such that $\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2=\mathrm{c}^2$. What is the largest possible prime factor of $\overline{a b c}$ ?
questions.solution:
a,b,c form Pythagoras triplet
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{abc}=345 \text { or } 435 \\
& 345=3 \times 5 \times 23 \text { and } 435=5 \times 3 \times 29
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow$ Largest possible prime factor $=29$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A pen costs Rs. 13 and a note book costs Rs. 17. A school spends exactly Rs. 10000 in the year 2017-18 to buy $x$ pens and $y$ note books such that $x$ and $y$ are as close as possible (i.e. $|x-y|$ is minimum). Next year, in 2018-19, the school spends a little more than Rs. 10000 and buys y pens and $x$ note books. How much more did the school pay?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 13 x+17 y=10000 \\ & 17 x+13 y=10000+a\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{10000-17 y}{13} \\ & y=329 \\ & x=339 \\ & \Rightarrow 10040\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let the rational number $p / q$ be closest to but not equal to $22 / 7$ among all rational numbers with denominator $ < 100$. What is the value of $p-3 q$ ?
questions.solution:
We have to have $\left|\frac{22}{7}-\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{q}}\right|$ to be as small as possible for $\mathrm{q} < 100$. $\left|\frac{22}{7}-\frac{p}{q}\right|=\left|\frac{22 q-7 p}{7 q}\right|$ should be smallest.
$\Rightarrow|22 q-7 p|=1$
and $q=99$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow p=311 \\ & p-3 q=311-3 \times 99=311-297=14\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Consider all 6-digit numbers of the form abccba where b is odd. Determine the number of all such 6-digit numbers that are divisible by 7 .
questions.solution:
abccba ( b is odd)
$\begin{aligned}
& =a\left(10^5+1\right)+b\left(10^4+10\right)+c\left(10^3+10^2\right) \\
& =a(1001-1) 100+a+10 b(1001)+(100)(11) c \\
& =(7.11 .13 .100) a-99 a+10 b(7.11 .13)+(98+2)(11) c \\
& =7 p+(c-a) \text { where } p \text { is an integer }
\end{aligned}$
Now if $\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a}$ is a multiple of 7
$c-a=7,0,-7$
Hence number of ordered pairs of (a, c) is 14
since b is odd, Number of such number $=14 \times 5=70$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let N be the smallest positive integer such that $\mathrm{N}+2 \mathrm{~N}+3 \mathrm{~N}+\ldots+9 \mathrm{~N}$ is a number all whose digits are equal. What is the sum of the digits of $N$ ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{N} \in \mathrm{Z}^{+} \\
& \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{N}+2 \mathrm{~N}+3 \mathrm{~N}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+9 \mathrm{~N}=45 \mathrm{~N} \\
& 45=\underbrace{555 \ldots \ldots \ldots . .5}_{\text {a times }}
\end{aligned}$

Only $55 \ldots \ldots \ldots .5$ can be repeated as 45 N will have units place $=0$ or 5
Also as $45 \mathrm{~N} \rightarrow$ multiple of ' 9 ' also.
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { If } a=9 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{555555555}{45}=N \\
& \therefore N=12345679
\end{aligned}$

Sum of digits of $\mathrm{N}=37$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A book is published in three volumes, the pages being numbered from 1 onwards. The page numbers are continued from the first volume to the second volume to the third. The number of pages in the second volume is 50 more than that in the first volume, and the number pages in the third volume is one and a half times that in the second. The sum of the page numbers on the first pages of the three volumes is 1709. If $n$ is the last page number, what is the largest prime factor of n ?
questions.solution:
Let the number of pages in volume-1 be x
$\therefore$ Number of pages in second volume $=\mathrm{x}+50$
$\therefore$ Number of pages in third volume $=\frac{3}{2}(x+50)$
Moreover $1+(x+1)+(2 x+51)=1709$
$\Rightarrow 3 x+53=1709=\Rightarrow x=552$
So $\mathrm{n}=552+602+903=2057$
So $\mathrm{n}=11^2 \times 17$
Hence largest prime factor of $\mathrm{n}=17$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Find the number of positive integers less than 101 that can not be written as the difference of two squares of integers.
questions.solution:
Let, $\mathrm{c} \rightarrow$ be positive integer $\leq 100$ such that $\mathrm{c}^2=\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2 ; \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{Z}$.
$\mathbf{C}-\mathbf{I}$ : Difference of a and b is 1 .
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & 1^2-0^2=1 \\ & 2^2-1^2=3 \\ & 3^2-2^2=5 \\ & 4^2-3^2=7 \\ & 5^2-4^2=9 \\ & \vdots \\ & 50^2-49^2=99\end{array}$

All odd number upto 100 can be expressed as difference of two squares. $\mathbf{C}-\boldsymbol{I I}$ : Difference of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ is 2
$\begin{aligned} & 2^2-0^2=4 \\ & 3^2-1^2=8 \\ & 4^2-2^2=12 \\ & \vdots \\ & 26^2-24^2=100\end{aligned}$
All multiples of ' 4 ' upto 100 can be expressed as difference of two squares.
$\mathbf{C}$ - III : Difference of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ is 3
This case will give odd numbers which are already counted
$\mathbf{C}$ - IV : Difference of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ is 4
This case will give multiples of 4 which are already counted
Similarly, the remaining cases will all give number already counted in
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{II}$
$\therefore \quad$ No. of numbers which can be expressed as difference of squares
$=($ odd numbers $)+($ multiples of 4$)=50+25=75$
$\therefore \quad$ Required numbers which cannot be expressed as a difference of squares of two integers are $=100-75=25$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $N$ denote the number of all natural numbers $n$ such that $n$ is divisible by a prime $p>\sqrt{n}$ and $\mathrm{p} < 20$. What is the value of N ?

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $a$ and $b$ natural numbers such that $2 a-b, a-2 b$ and $a+b$ are all distinct squares. What is the smallest possible value of $b$ ?
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{k}_1^2$......(1)
$a-2 b=k_2^2$......(2)
$a+b=k_3{ }^2$......(3)
Add (2) \& (3) we get
$2 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{k}_2^2+\mathrm{k}_3^2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_2^2+\mathrm{k}_3^2=\mathrm{k}_1^2 \quad\left(\mathrm{k}_2 < \mathrm{k}_3\right)$
For least ' b ' difference of $\mathrm{k}_3{ }^2$ $\&$ $\mathrm{k}_2{ }^2$ is also least and must be multiple of 3
$\Rightarrow  \mathrm{k}_2^2=\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a}^2$ & $\mathrm{k}_3^2=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=12^2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_3^2-\mathrm{k}_2^2=3 \mathrm{~b}=144-81=63 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=21$
$\Rightarrow$ least $b$ is 21

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let T be the smallest positive integers which, when divided by $11,13,15$ leaves remainders in the sets $\{7,8,9\},\{1,2,3\},\{4,5,6\}$ respectively. What is the sum of the square of the digits of $T$ ?
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: If $a, b, c \geq 4$ are integers, not all equal, and $4 a b c=(a+3)(b+3)(c+3)$, then what is the value of $a+b+c ?$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 4 a b c=27+3(a b+b c+c a)+9(a+b+c)+a b c \\
& \Rightarrow 3 a b c=27+3(a b+b c+c a)+9(a+b+c) \\
& \Rightarrow  a b c=9+(a b+b c+c a)+3(a+b+c) \\
& \Rightarrow  a b c-(a b+b c+c a)+(a+b+c)-1=8+4(a+b+c) \\
& \Rightarrow (a-1)(b-1)(c-1)=8+4(a+b+c)
\end{aligned}$
Put $\mathrm{a}-1=\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{b}-1=\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{c}-1=\mathrm{C}$,
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & A B C=20+4(A+B+C) \\
\Rightarrow & A=\frac{4(5+B+C)}{B C-4} \\
\Rightarrow & B=3, C=4, A=6
\end{array}$
or they can be interchanged
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & (a, b, c) \text { are anangemets of }(4,5,7) \\
\Rightarrow & a+b+c=16
\end{array}$
